sections.title: What is the Imperative?
sections.description: The Imperative is the grammatical mood used to give commands, requests, advice, or instructions. In German, the form of the verb changes depending on who you are talking to.

forms.title: There are three main forms of the Imperative:
forms.form_1: 1. Formell (Formal): Addressing one or more people formally (Sie).
forms.form_2: 2. Informell Singular (Informal): Addressing one friend, child, or family member (du).
forms.form_3: 3. Informell Plural (Informal): Addressing two or more friends or children (ihr).

sections.title: 1. How to Form the Imperative
sections.description: The imperative form depends on the pronoun and context.

rows: Use the Infinitive + "Sie". The word order flips.
Use the "ihr" form of the verb, but drop the pronoun.
Use the "du" form, but drop the "st" ending and the pronoun.

sections.title: 2. Specialized Verb Categories
sections.description: Not all verbs follow the basic pattern perfectly. Here is how we handle irregulars:

rows: A. Verbs with Stem Changes (e → i)
If a verb changes from e to i in the present tense, the singular informal imperative keeps that change.
Unlike the e → i verbs, verbs that get an umlaut in the "du" form lose the umlaut in the imperative.
The prefix always moves to the very end of the sentence.
The verb sein (to be) is unique and must be memorized.
The verb haben (to have) is unique and must be memorized.

sections.title: 3. Softening the Command (Politeness)
sections.description: Commands can sound harsh in German. To make them sound like friendly advice or polite requests, we add specific words:

rows: Can be placed at the beginning or end.
Helfen Sie mir bitte! (Help me, please!)
Makes the command sound more natural and less harsh.
Komm mal her! (Just come over here for a second!)
Softens the command, makes it sound like encouragement.
Probier doch den Kuchen! (Go ahead and try the cake!)
Combination that sounds very natural and friendly.
Mach doch mal Pause! (Take a break sometime!)

sections.title: 4. Practical Examples for Everyday Situations
sections.description: Here is how you would use these rules in different contexts:

rows: A teacher speaking to a whole class.
A teacher speaking to a whole class.
Offering a seat to a stranger.
Telling a friend not to be afraid.
Don't have any fear/Don't be afraid!

sections.title: Summary Checklist for Students
sections.description: Quick reference for forming the imperative correctly:

checklist: Identify your audience: Is it Sie, du, or ihr?
Sie: Keep the "n" and keep the "Sie".
du: Delete the "st" ending, delete "du", and check if the "e" needs to change to "i".
